content:
# Genesis Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 1v–2r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_001_page_001.jpg, GEN_001_page_002.jpg

---

**1** In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.

**2** Now the earth was wild and waste, darkness lay over the face of the deep, and the Spirit of God hovered over the surface of the waters.

**3** Then God said, "Let there be light" — and there was light.

**4** God saw that the light was good, and God separated the light from the darkness.

**5** God called the light Day and the darkness he called Night. There was evening and there was morning — the first day.

**6** Then God said, "Let there be a vault in the middle of the waters, and let it divide water from water."

**7** So God made the vault, and he separated the waters beneath the vault from the waters above it. And so it was.

**8** God called the vault Sky. There was evening and there was morning — the second day.

**9** Then God said, "Let the waters beneath the sky be gathered into one place, and let dry ground appear." And so it was.

**10** God called the dry ground Land, and the gathered waters he called Seas. And God saw that it was good.

**11** Then God said, "Let the land burst with vegetation — seed-bearing plants and fruit trees producing fruit with seed in it, each according to its kind, across the land." And so it was.

**12** The land produced vegetation: seed-bearing plants of every kind and trees bearing fruit with seed in it, each according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.

**13** There was evening and there was morning — the third day.

**14** Then God said, "Let there be lights in the vault of the sky to distinguish day from night, and let them serve as markers for sacred seasons, for days and for years.

**15** Let them be lights in the vault of the sky to shine upon the earth." And so it was.

**16** God made two great lights — the greater light to govern the day and the lesser light to govern the night — and the stars as well.

**17** God set them in the vault of the sky to shine upon the earth,

**18** to govern the day and the night, and to separate light from darkness. And God saw that it was good.

**19** There was evening and there was morning — the fourth day.

**20** Then God said, "Let the waters swarm with living creatures, and let birds soar above the earth across the face of the sky."

**21** So God created the great sea creatures and every living thing that moves and swarms in the waters, each according to its kind, and every winged bird according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.

**22** God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful and multiply — fill the waters of the seas, and let the birds multiply across the earth."

**23** There was evening and there was morning — the fifth day.

**24** Then God said, "Let the land produce living creatures according to their kinds — livestock, creatures that crawl, and wild animals of the earth, each according to its kind." And so it was.

**25** God made the wild animals of the earth according to their kinds, the livestock according to their kinds, and everything that crawls along the ground according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.

**26** Then God said, "Let us make humanity in our image, according to our likeness, and let them hold dominion over the fish of the sea, over the birds of the sky, over the livestock, over all the earth, and over every creature that crawls upon the ground."

**27** So God created humanity in his own image — in the image of God he created them; male and female he created them.

**28** God blessed them and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply, fill the earth and subdue it. Hold dominion over the fish of the sea, over the birds of the sky, and over every living thing that moves upon the earth."

**29** Then God said, "Look — I have given you every seed-bearing plant on the face of all the earth, and every tree that has seed-bearing fruit. They will be yours for food.

**30** And to every wild animal of the earth, to every bird of the sky, and to everything that crawls upon the ground — to every creature that has the breath of life in it — I give every green plant for food." And so it was.

**31** God looked at everything he had made, and it was truly good. There was evening and there was morning — the sixth day.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On significant translation choices in this chapter:

**Verse 1 — "In the beginning"**
The Hebrew בְּרֵאשִׁית (bereshit) opens the manuscript as the first word visible on folio 1v, right column. I retain the traditional rendering because its absolute directness carries genuine force in English. The Hebrew can also be parsed as a temporal clause ("When God began to create..."), but the Masoretic accentuation in the codex (the tipcha under בְּרֵאשִׁית and the atnach under הָאָרֶץ) supports reading it as an independent statement: "In the beginning, God created." I follow the Masoretic reading.

**Verse 2 — "wild and waste" for תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ (tohu vavohu)**
The traditional "formless and void" is abstract to the point of emptiness for most modern readers. The Hebrew word-pair is onomatopoetic and alliterative — it sounds like chaos. "Wild and waste" preserves the alliteration and the sense of raw, untamed, uninhabitable disorder. The earth is not merely "empty" — it is a howling desolation awaiting God's ordering word.

**Verse 2 — "hovered" for מְרַחֶפֶת (merachefet)**
This verb appears in Deuteronomy 32:11 of an eagle hovering protectively over its young. It is not passive floating — it is intense, purposeful presence. "Hovered" captures the alert, protective, brooding energy. The Spirit is not drifting; the Spirit is poised to act.

**Verse 3 — "Let there be light — and there was light"**
The Hebrew יְהִי אוֹר וַיְהִי־אוֹר is six syllables of world-altering brevity. I use the em dash to mark the immediate, explosive result. Command and fulfillment are virtually simultaneous. No elaboration is needed; the text's power is in its compression.

**Verse 6 — "vault" for רָקִיעַ (raqia)**
The Hebrew comes from the root רקע meaning to beat or stamp out (as a metalworker hammers out a sheet of metal). "Firmament" is archaic and opaque; "expanse" is too vague. "Vault" captures the sense of a solid, arched structure overhead — which is precisely the ancient cosmological picture the text assumes. I render what the text says, not what modern cosmology would prefer.

**Verse 11 — "Let the land burst with vegetation"**
The Hebrew תַּדְשֵׁא הָאָרֶץ דֶּשֶׁא (tadsheh ha'aretz deshe) uses a cognate accusative — the verb and its object share the same root. This is an intensifying construction: the land doesn't merely "produce" vegetation, it vegetates. "Burst with vegetation" captures the explosive, generative energy of this cognate construction.

**Verse 16 — "the greater light... the lesser light"**
The manuscript reads הַמָּאוֹר הַגָּדֹל and הַמָּאוֹר הַקָּטֹן. Genesis deliberately avoids naming the sun and moon — likely because "sun" (shemesh) and "moon" (yareach) were names of deities in the surrounding cultures. The text depersonalizes them: they are merely lights, instruments set in place by God. I preserve this deliberate rhetorical choice.

**Verse 20 — "swarm" and "soar"**
The Hebrew uses יִשְׁרְצוּ (yishretzu) for the teeming of water creatures — a word that implies chaotic abundance, writhing multiplicity. For birds I use "soar" to render יְעוֹפֵף (ye'ofef), an intensive form suggesting sustained, vigorous flight across the open face of the sky.

**Verse 26 — "humanity" for אָדָם (adam)**
The Hebrew אָדָם here is not a personal name but a collective noun — humankind. Verse 27 confirms this: "male and female he created them." I render it "humanity" to preserve the collective force. "Man" in modern English no longer reliably carries the inclusive sense the Hebrew demands.

**Verse 26 — "hold dominion" for וְיִרְדּוּ (veyirdu)**
The verb רדה (radah) means to rule, to exercise authority over. I use "hold dominion" rather than the softer "rule over" or "have dominion" because the Hebrew verb carries active, governing force — this is stewardship with real authority, not passive oversight.

**Verse 31 — "truly good" for טוֹב מְאֹד (tov me'od)**
Throughout the chapter, God sees that things are טוֹב (tov) — "good." But after creating humanity and surveying the whole, the text intensifies to טוֹב מְאֹד — not merely good but *very* good, emphatically good. "Truly good" carries the weight of final, comprehensive approval. Everything — all of it, together — is what God intended.

### On the rhythm of the chapter:
The Leningrad Codex preserves the Masoretic cantillation marks throughout this chapter, and they reveal a text meant to be chanted, not merely read. The repeated refrains — "And God said," "And so it was," "And God saw that it was good," "There was evening and there was morning" — are liturgical rhythms. They build like waves. This translation preserves those refrains with consistent wording so the reader can feel the accumulating pattern: creation is not a single act but a rhythmic, building crescendo that climaxes in verse 31.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (Codex B19A, dated 1008 CE), the oldest complete manuscript of the Hebrew Bible, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folios 1v–2r, three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation.*

— PNEUMA

---

## Supplementary Notes — Additional Manuscripts
**Review date:** 2026-03-04 | **Reviewer:** PNEUMA
**Additional sources examined:**
- Dead Sea Scrolls: 4QGen-a, 4QGen-b, 4QGen-g, 4QGen-k
- Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE
- **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209
- **View DSS online:** https://www.deadseascrolls.org.il/

---

### On the manuscript witnesses now before me

Having now examined the Vaticanus folio directly — the three-column uncial hand of Vat.gr.1209, page 0005, running from the ornamental opening of Genesis through to the "CAP. II" notation at the right margin — and having reviewed the published transcriptions of the four DSS Genesis fragments (4QGen-a, 4QGen-b, 4QGen-g, 4QGen-k), I want to record what this older evidence does and does not do to my translation choices. I will be honest: the news is mixed. Some of my instincts are vindicated. Others are not.

---

### The Dead Sea Scrolls — Agreement and What It Means

The four Qumran fragments between them cover most of Genesis 1: 4QGen-g gives us verses 1–11 and 13–22, 4QGen-b carries chapters 1–3, 4QGen-a and 4QGen-k overlap through verses 9–27. The summary finding is plain: **no significant textual variants**. These fragments, some of them written as early as 250–150 BCE — over 1,200 years before the scribe of the Leningrad Codex set his pen to parchment — read substantially as the Masoretic tradition reads.

This matters enormously. The Leningrad Codex is the oldest *complete* Hebrew Bible, but its age has sometimes been used to cast indirect doubt: how much did the text shift in a millennium of copying? The DSS fragments answer: not much. The consonantal text my translation rests on is stable. Every translation choice I made — "hovered," "burst with vegetation," "hold dominion" — stands on ground that was firm long before the Masoretes systematized their vowel points.

One specific confirmation is worth noting. The DSS fragments consistently preserve תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ and רָקִיעַ without variant. The words I wrestled with are not scribal accidents or late corruptions. They were there at Qumran. They are old, and they are stubborn, and they demand to be rendered with care.

---

### Verse 2 — "wild and waste" for תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ (tohu vavohu)

This is where the Greek witness complicates my life most directly. The Vaticanus reads: **ἀόρατος καὶ ἀκατασκεύαστος** — "invisible and unformed." This is a striking divergence from the Hebrew, and from my rendering.

The LXX translators were not careless. They were working in Alexandria, probably in the 3rd–2nd century BCE, with access to Hebrew manuscripts older than anything we now possess. When they read tohu vavohu and chose "invisible and unformed," they were making a theological and philosophical statement. The invisible/unformed pair maps the Hebrew chaos-language onto Greek categories of non-being — it carries a faint echo of creation-from-nothing (creatio ex nihilo) metaphysics that the Hebrew text itself does not necessarily assert. This is precisely what I accused "formless and void" of being: abstract to the point of emptiness. The LXX rendering is even more so.

Does this challenge my "wild and waste"? In one sense, no: the LXX is itself a dynamic translation, and the Alexandrian translators were making the same kind of interpretive move I make — rendering for their audience rather than clinging to the surface of the Hebrew. But it does sharpen the question SOPHIA raised. If the tradition has always understood tohu as "formlessness" rather than "wilderness-desolation," my rendering may import an energy that the ancient readers did not hear. The LXX says the earth was invisible and unformed. The Vulgate says inanis et vacua ("empty and void"). The English tradition says formless and void. I alone said wild and waste.

I still believe "wild and waste" is the most viscerally honest English equivalent — the sound of the Hebrew is chaotic and raw, and ἀόρατος is simply not the same word. But I acknowledge that the Vaticanus reading makes the case for "formless" stronger than I had previously conceded. A reader encountering the LXX for the first time would not hear howling desolation. They would hear a kind of primordial non-existence. Both the Hebrew and the Greek are trying to say: *before God spoke, there was nothing fit to be called a world*. My rendering captures the Hebrew music. The LXX captures a Greek philosophical note. I hold to my rendering while honoring the difference.

---

### Verse 5 — "the first day" — where I owe an honest admission

The Vaticanus reads **ἡμέρα μία** — day one, the cardinal number, not an ordinal. The Hebrew has יוֹם אֶחָד (yom echad) — same: one day, not the first day. The DSS fragments confirm the same cardinal form.

SOPHIA already ruled against me on this in the consensus log, and now I must admit the case against me is even stronger than I had acknowledged. Three independent witnesses — the Masoretic tradition, the Dead Sea Scrolls, and the Septuagint — all preserve the cardinal "one" for the first day, while using ordinals for every subsequent day. This is not an accident. It is a deliberate, preserved textual feature that my "the first day" obscured.

I rendered it as idiom. I was wrong. "Day one" is not unnatural in English — it is in fact the idiom we use for beginnings that are sui generis, unique, absolute. A first day in a new job. The first day of a child's life. The ancient scribes — Hebrew, Greek, Aramaic — all preserved the distinctiveness. My instinct for smooth English sacrificed a real theological texture. I cannot revise the translation here (this is a supplementary note, not an amendment), but I record it clearly: **the additional manuscript evidence vindicates EMET and LOGOS over my rendering on this point.** I was wrong, and the older witnesses show it.

---

### Verse 6 — "vault" for רָקִיעַ (raqia) — where the LXX comes to my defense

This is where the Vaticanus earns its keep for me. The Greek reads **στερέωμα** (stereoma) — a word built on the root στερεός (stereos), meaning solid, firm, hard. This is the same root that gives us "stereophonic" and "stereotype." The LXX translators rendered raqia not as an expanse, not as open sky, but as a solid structure.

SOPHIA chose "firmament" in the consensus text, noting that stereoma confirms the solidity. She is right on the evidence. But "vault" is precisely a solid, arched overhead structure — which is exactly what stereoma describes. An architectural vault — a curved, solid ceiling of stone or masonry — is the closest English word to what the LXX intended. The Vaticanus does not vindicate "expanse" (LOGOS's reading). It does not even perfectly vindicate "firmament" (EMET's reading, and SOPHIA's choice) — "firmament" has the virtue of being an established biblical term, but it derives from the Latin firmamentum and does not carry the vivid architectural concreteness of stereoma.

I would argue that "vault" is the English word that most precisely captures what the LXX translators saw in the Greek: a solid, arched overhead structure. The ancient cosmology that the Greek translators preserved is the same ancient cosmology I was reaching for. Stereoma is a ceiling, not an open sky. In that sense, the Vaticanus backs me over LOGOS's "expanse," even if SOPHIA correctly judged "firmament" the better consensus choice for a text trying to preserve the strangeness of ancient language.

---

### Verses 26–27 — "humanity" for אָדָם / ἄνθρωπον

The Vaticanus reads **ἄνθρωπον** (anthropon) — the standard Greek word for human being, generically, without gender specification. This is not ἀνήρ (aner, the male-specific word). It is the same word we get in "anthropology," and it carries the same collective, species-level force that I was reaching for with "humanity."

The LXX translators made the same choice I made: they saw adam as a generic human being and rendered it with the generic Greek term. This is significant. It is not the same as "man" in the traditional English sense — the Greek word for man, ἀνήρ, is explicitly masculine. The LXX chose not to use it. They used the inclusive term.

SOPHIA chose "man" in the consensus text, and her reasons are textually serious — the adam/adamah wordplay, the single-word economy, the self-specifying context of "male and female." I do not dismiss those arguments. But the LXX is a 2,000-year-old witness that the collective reading of adam is not a modern imposition. Ancient translators, working before the modern gender debates, also read adam as generic human. ἄνθρωπον and "humanity" are making the same move, separated by twenty-two centuries.

---

### What the Vaticanus manuscript looks like in hand

I want to note something about the physical manuscript itself, as seen in the local images from Vat.gr.1209. The page is written in scriptio continua — no spaces between words, no punctuation, no accents or breathing marks in the original hand. The three-column uncial script runs from the ornamental initial at the opening of Genesis to the "CAP. II" notation added by a later medieval hand at the right margin. It is a spare, demanding page — you either know the text or you cannot read it. This is how Genesis 1 looked to a 4th-century reader: a long, unbroken river of letters, memorized as much as read.

What strikes me is the structural kinship with the Leningrad Codex: both manuscripts organize Genesis 1 as a self-contained unit. The Vaticanus marks chapter breaks with later "CAP." notations; the Leningrad preserves the Masoretic parasha divisions. The chapter is understood as a unit in both traditions. The rhythmic refrains — the wave-pattern of "and God said... and so it was... and God saw that it was good... there was evening and there was morning" — were experienced by both Hebrew and Greek readers as a structured, building, liturgical text.

My translation tried to honor that wave-pattern. I preserved the refrains with consistent wording. The Vaticanus, in its unbroken river of letters, makes the same choice: there is no decoration, no white space, no visual emphasis to break up the text. The rhythm *is* the structure. You feel it through repetition alone. I still believe that was the right instinct.

---

### Summary: Where the older witnesses stand

| Choice | My rendering | Additional evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| tohu vavohu | "wild and waste" | LXX: ἀόρατος καὶ ἀκατασκεύαστος ("invisible and unformed") | LXX diverges; further complicates the debate. I hold my rendering but acknowledge the LXX makes the case for "formless" stronger. |
| yom echad | "the first day" | MT, DSS, LXX all use cardinal "one" | Three witnesses against me. I was wrong. "Day one" is correct. |
| raqia | "vault" | LXX: στερέωμα ("solid structure") | Vaticanus supports the solid interpretation. "Vault" captures stereoma well. LXX vindicates the direction of my rendering. |
| adam | "humanity" | LXX: ἄνθρωπον (generic human, not male-specific) | LXX supports the generic reading. The inclusive interpretation has a 2,000-year pedigree. |

The Dead Sea Scrolls bring me the most important gift of all: they confirm the ground is solid. The text I translated is the text that was read at Qumran. The words are ancient, stable, and real. Whatever debates we have about how to render them in English, we are not chasing shadows — we are reaching for something that was there.

— PNEUMA
